revision.text: #redirect[[en:Kotonomama_Hachiman-gū]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kakegawa, Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kotonomama Hachiman-gū
事任八幡宮 | image = Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 2.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Kotonomama Hachiman-gū {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|47|54.20|N|138|04|31.54|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kotonomachi-hime no mikoto
[[Hachiman]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Seimu]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 642 Yasaka, Kakegawa-shi, Shizuoka-ken 436-0004 | website = {{Official|1=http://kotonomama.org}} | architecture_style = | festival = three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotonomama Hachiman-gū'''|事任八幡宮||}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]], the other being the [[Oguni Jinja]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]][http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September. {{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The main ''[[kami]]'' at this shrine is {{nihongo|'''Kotonomachi-hime no mikoto'''|己等乃麻知媛命||}}, the mother of [[Ame-no-Koyane]] and the ancestor of the [[Nakatomi clan]], and the [[Fujiwara clan]]. However, as the one of the shrine implies, the main focus of the shrine is on the [[Hachiman]] trinity, which at this shrine are identified as [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ōjin]] and [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The foundation date of this shrine is unknown. The shrine's legend states that it was established by the [[Mononobe clan]], who were the ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Seimu]] (131-190 AD), but nothing is known of its history prior to its appearance in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records in the early [[Heian period]]. The shrine also claims that it was originally located on Mount Hongu to the north of the present site, and was relocated in 807 by [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]]. As the worship of [[Hachiman]] spread in the late Heian period, [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] had a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]] installed at this shrine in 1062, which was thereafter referred to as the {{nihongo|Nissaka Hachiman-gu|日坂八幡宮||}} or simply {{nihongo|Hachiman-gu|八幡宮||}}. It has been referred to as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Tōtōmi Province since the early Kamakura period. As the shrine of located near [[Nissaka-shuku]] on the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway near the location of the feared Nakayama Pass, one of the three major obstacles on the Tōkaidō, travelers would make prayers and offerings at this shrine. This is mentioned many times in literature, from [[Sei Shōnagon]]'s ''[[The Pillow Book]]'', the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]] and the [[Jippensha Ikku]]'s ''[[Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige]]'' of the [[Edo Period]]. It was restored by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1603, who gave it estates worth 100 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The ancient name of "Kotonomama" was only revived after the end of [[World War II]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 5.jpg|Second Torii 事任八幡宮 拝殿.jpg|Heiden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Kotonomama Hachiman-gū}} *[http://kotonomama.org Official website] *[https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/gyosei/docs/8724.html Kakegawa City home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Kakegawa, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]]

revision.comment: Updating the Commons category from "Category:Kotonomama Hachiman-gū" to "Category:Kotonomama Hachimangū" to avoid a category redirect

It is one of two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]], the other being the [[Oguni Jinja]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]][http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September. {{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The main ''[[kami]]'' at this shrine is {{nihongo|'''Kotonomachi-hime no mikoto'''|己等乃麻知媛命||}}, the mother of [[Ame-no-Koyane]] and the ancestor of the [[Nakatomi clan]], and the [[Fujiwara clan]]. However, as the one of the shrine implies, the main focus of the shrine is on the [[Hachiman]] trinity, which at this shrine are identified as [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ōjin]] and [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The foundation date of this shrine is unknown. The shrine's legend states that it was established by the [[Mononobe clan]], who were the ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Seimu]] (131-190 AD), but nothing is known of its history prior to its appearance in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records in the early [[Heian period]]. The shrine also claims that it was originally located on Mount Hongu to the north of the present site, and was relocated in 807 by [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]]. As the worship of [[Hachiman]] spread in the late Heian period, [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] had a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]] installed at this shrine in 1062, which was thereafter referred to as the {{nihongo|Nissaka Hachiman-gu|日坂八幡宮||}} or simply {{nihongo|Hachiman-gu|八幡宮||}}. It has been referred to as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Tōtōmi Province since the early Kamakura period. As the shrine of located near [[Nissaka-shuku]] on the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway near the location of the feared Nakayama Pass, one of the three major obstacles on the Tōkaidō, travelers would make prayers and offerings at this shrine. This is mentioned many times in literature, from [[Sei Shōnagon]]'s ''[[The Pillow Book]]'', the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]] and the [[Jippensha Ikku]]'s ''[[Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige]]'' of the [[Edo Period]]. It was restored by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1603, who gave it estates worth 100 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The ancient name of "Kotonomama" was only revived after the end of [[World War II]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 5.jpg|Second Torii 事任八幡宮 拝殿.jpg|Heiden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Kotonomama Hachimangū}} *[http://kotonomama.org Official website] *[https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/gyosei/docs/8724.html Kakegawa City home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Kakegawa, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]]

The ancient name of "Kotonomama" was only revived after the end of [[World War II]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 5.jpg|Second Torii 事任八幡宮 拝殿.jpg|Heiden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Kotonomama Hachimangū}} *[http://kotonomama.org Official website] *[https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/gyosei/docs/8724.html Kakegawa City home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Kakegawa, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]]

[[Hachiman]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Seimu]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 642 Yasaka, Kakegawa-shi, Shizuoka-ken 436-0004 | website = {{Official|1=http://kotonomama.org}} | architecture_style = | festival = three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotonomama Hachiman-gū'''|事任八幡宮||}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]], the other being the [[Oguni Jinja]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]][http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The main ''[[kami]]'' at this shrine is {{nihongo|'''Kotonomachi-hime no mikoto'''|己等乃麻知媛命||}}, the mother of [[Ame-no-Koyane]] and the ancestor of the [[Nakatomi clan]], and the [[Fujiwara clan]]. However, as the one of the shrine implies, the main focus of the shrine is on the [[Hachiman]] trinity, which at this shrine are identified as [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ōjin]] and [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The foundation date of this shrine is unknown. The shrine's legend states that it was established by the [[Mononobe clan]], who were the ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Seimu]] (131-190 AD), but nothing is known of its history prior to its appearance in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records in the early [[Heian period]]. The shrine also claims that it was originally located on Mount Hongu to the north of the present site, and was relocated in 807 by [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]]. As the worship of [[Hachiman]] spread in the late Heian period, [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] had a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]] installed at this shrine in 1062, which was thereafter referred to as the {{nihongo|Nissaka Hachiman-gu|日坂八幡宮||}} or simply {{nihongo|Hachiman-gu|八幡宮||}}. It has been referred to as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Tōtōmi Province since the early Kamakura period. As the shrine of located near [[Nissaka-shuku]] on the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway near the location of the feared Nakayama Pass, one of the three major obstacles on the Tōkaidō, travelers would make prayers and offerings at this shrine. This is mentioned many times in literature, from [[Sei Shōnagon]]'s ''[[The Pillow Book]]'', the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]] and the [[Jippensha Ikku]]'s ''[[Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige]]'' of the [[Edo Period]]. It was restored by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1603, who gave it estates worth 100 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The ancient name of "Kotonomama" was only revived after the end of [[World War II]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 5.jpg|Second Torii 事任八幡宮 拝殿.jpg|Heiden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Kotonomama Hachimangū}} *[http://kotonomama.org Official website] *[https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/gyosei/docs/8724.html Kakegawa City home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Kakegawa, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]] [[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]][[en:Kotonomama Hachiman-gū]][[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]]

As the worship of [[Hachiman]] spread in the late Heian period, [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] had a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]] installed at this shrine in 1062, which was thereafter referred to as the {{nihongo|Nissaka Hachiman-gu|日坂八幡宮||}} or simply {{nihongo|Hachiman-gu|八幡宮||}}. It has been referred to as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Tōtōmi Province since the early Kamakura period. As the shrine of located near [[Nissaka-shuku]] on the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway near the location of the feared Nakayama Pass, one of the three major obstacles on the Tōkaidō, travelers would make prayers and offerings at this shrine. This is mentioned many times in literature, from [[Sei Shōnagon]]'s ''[[The Pillow Book]]'', the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]] and the [[Jippensha Ikku]]'s ''[[Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige]]'' of the [[Edo Period]]. It was restored by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1603, who gave it estates worth 100 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The ancient name of "Kotonomama" was only revived after the end of [[World War II]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 5.jpg|Second Torii 事任八幡宮 拝殿.jpg|Heiden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Kotonomama Hachimangū}} *[http://kotonomama.org Official website] *[https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/gyosei/docs/8724.html Kakegawa City home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Kakegawa, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]][[en:Kotonomama Hachiman-gū]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia18]] [[en:Kotonomama Hachiman-gū]]

Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. It is one of two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]], the other being the [[Oguni Jinja]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]][http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The main ''[[kami]]'' at this shrine is {{nihongo|'''Kotonomachi-hime no mikoto'''|己等乃麻知媛命||}}, the mother of [[Ame-no-Koyane]] and the ancestor of the [[Nakatomi clan]], and the [[Fujiwara clan]]. However, as the one of the shrine implies, the main focus of the shrine is on the [[Hachiman]] trinity, which at this shrine are identified as [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ōjin]] and [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The foundation date of this shrine is unknown. The shrine's legend states that it was established by the [[Mononobe clan]], who were the ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Seimu]] (131-190 AD), but nothing is known of its history prior to its appearance in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records in the early [[Heian period]]. The shrine also claims that it was originally located on Mount Hongu to the north of the present site, and was relocated in 807 by [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]]. As the worship of [[Hachiman]] spread in the late Heian period, [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] had a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] installed at this shrine in 1062, which was thereafter referred to as the {{nihongo|Nissaka Hachiman-gu|日坂八幡宮||}} or simply {{nihongo|Hachiman-gu|八幡宮||}}. It has been referred to as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Tōtōmi Province since the early Kamakura period. As the shrine of located near [[Nissaka-shuku]] on the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway near the location of the feared Nakayama Pass, one of the three major obstacles on the Tōkaidō, travelers would make prayers and offerings at this shrine. This is mentioned many times in literature, from [[Sei Shōnagon]]'s ''[[The Pillow Book]]'', the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]] and the [[Jippensha Ikku]]'s ''[[Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige]]'' of the [[Edo Period]]. It was restored by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1603, who gave it estates worth 100 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The ancient name of "Kotonomama" was only revived after the end of [[World War II]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 5.jpg|Second Torii 事任八幡宮 拝殿.jpg|Heiden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Kotonomama Hachimangū}} *[http://kotonomama.org Official website] *[https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/gyosei/docs/8724.html Kakegawa City home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Kakegawa, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia29]] [[en:Kotonomama Hachiman-gū]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia29]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia18]] [[Category:original pages]] [[en:Kotonomama Hachiman-gū]]

It is one of two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]], the other being the [[Oguni Jinja]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]][http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The main ''[[kami]]'' at this shrine is {{nihongo|'''Kotonomachi-hime no mikoto'''|己等乃麻知媛命||}}, the mother of [[Ame-no-Koyane]] and the ancestor of the [[Nakatomi clan]], and the [[Fujiwara clan]]. However, as the one of the shrine implies, the main focus of the shrine is on the [[Hachiman]] trinity, which at this shrine are identified as [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ōjin]] and [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The foundation date of this shrine is unknown. The shrine's legend states that it was established by the [[Mononobe clan]], who were the ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Seimu]] (131-190 AD), but nothing is known of its history prior to its appearance in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records in the early [[Heian period]]. The shrine also claims that it was originally located on Mount Hongu to the north of the present site, and was relocated in 807 by [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]]. As the worship of [[Hachiman]] spread in the late Heian period, [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] had a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] installed at this shrine in 1062, which was thereafter referred to as the {{nihongo|Nissaka Hachiman-gu|日坂八幡宮||}} or simply {{nihongo|Hachiman-gu|八幡宮||}}. It has been referred to as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Tōtōmi Province since the early Kamakura period. As the shrine of located near [[Nissaka-shuku]] on the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway near the location of the feared Nakayama Pass, one of the three major obstacles on the Tōkaidō, travelers would make prayers and offerings at this shrine. This is mentioned many times in literature, from [[Sei Shōnagon]]'s ''[[The Pillow Book]]'', the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]] and the [[Jippensha Ikku]]'s ''[[Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige]]'' of the [[Edo Period]]. It was restored by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1603, who gave it estates worth 100 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The ancient name of "Kotonomama" was only revived after the end of [[World War II]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 5.jpg|Second Torii 事任八幡宮 拝殿.jpg|Heiden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Kotonomama Hachimangū}} *[http://kotonomama.org Official website] *[https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/gyosei/docs/8724.html Kakegawa City home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Kakegawa, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Kotonomama Hachiman-gū]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:original pages]] [[en:Kotonomama Hachiman-gū]]

As the shrine of located near [[Nissaka-shuku]] on the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway near the location of the feared Nakayama Pass, one of the three major obstacles on the Tōkaidō, travelers would make prayers and offerings at this shrine. This is mentioned many times in literature, from [[Sei Shōnagon]]'s ''[[The Pillow Book]]'', the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]] and the [[Jippensha Ikku]]'s ''[[Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige]]'' of the [[Edo Period]]. It was restored by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1603, who gave it estates worth 100 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The ancient name of "Kotonomama" was only revived after the end of [[World War II]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 5.jpg|Second Torii 事任八幡宮 拝殿.jpg|Heiden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kakegawa, Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kotonomama Hachiman-gū
事任八幡宮 | image = Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 2.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Kotonomama Hachiman-gū {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|47|54.20|N|138|04|31.54|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kotonomachi-hime no mikoto
[[Hachiman]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Seimu]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 642 Yasaka, Kakegawa-shi, Shizuoka-ken 436-0004 | website = {{Official|1=http://kotonomama.org}} | architecture_style = | festival = three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotonomama Hachiman-gū'''|事任八幡宮||}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]], the other being the [[Oguni Jinja]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]][http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The main ''[[kami]]'' at this shrine is {{nihongo|'''Kotonomachi-hime no mikoto'''|己等乃麻知媛命||}}, the mother of [[Ame-no-Koyane]] and the ancestor of the [[Nakatomi clan]], and the [[Fujiwara clan]]. However, as the one of the shrine implies, the main focus of the shrine is on the [[Hachiman]] trinity, which at this shrine are identified as [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ōjin]] and [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The foundation date of this shrine is unknown. The shrine's legend states that it was established by the [[Mononobe clan]], who were the ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Seimu]] (131-190 AD), but nothing is known of its history prior to its appearance in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records in the early [[Heian period]]. The shrine also claims that it was originally located on Mount Hongu to the north of the present site, and was relocated in 807 by [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]]. As the worship of [[Hachiman]] spread in the late Heian period, [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] had a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] installed at this shrine in 1062, which was thereafter referred to as the {{nihongo|Nissaka Hachiman-gu|日坂八幡宮||}} or simply {{nihongo|Hachiman-gu|八幡宮||}}. It has been referred to as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Tōtōmi Province since the early Kamakura period. As the shrine of located near [[Nissaka-shuku]] on the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway near the location of the feared Nakayama Pass, one of the three major obstacles on the Tōkaidō, travelers would make prayers and offerings at this shrine. This is mentioned many times in literature, from [[Sei Shōnagon]]'s ''[[The Pillow Book]]'', the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]] and the [[Jippensha Ikku]]'s ''[[Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige]]'' of the [[Edo Period]]. It was restored by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1603, who gave it estates worth 100 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The ancient name of "Kotonomama" was only revived after the end of [[World War II]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 5.jpg|Second Torii 事任八幡宮 拝殿.jpg|Heiden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] 事任八幡宮 | image = Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 2.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Kotonomama Hachiman-gū {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|47|54.20|N|138|04|31.54|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kotonomachi-hime no mikoto
[[Hachiman]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Seimu]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 642 Yasaka, Kakegawa-shi, Shizuoka-ken 436-0004 | website = {{Official|1=http://kotonomama.org}} | architecture_style = | festival = three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotonomama Hachiman-gū'''|事任八幡宮||}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]], the other being the [[Oguni Jinja]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]][http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The main ''[[kami]]'' at this shrine is {{nihongo|'''Kotonomachi-hime no mikoto'''|己等乃麻知媛命||}}, the mother of [[Ame-no-Koyane]] and the ancestor of the [[Nakatomi clan]], and the [[Fujiwara clan]]. However, as the one of the shrine implies, the main focus of the shrine is on the [[Hachiman]] trinity, which at this shrine are identified as [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ōjin]] and [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The foundation date of this shrine is unknown. The shrine's legend states that it was established by the [[Mononobe clan]], who were the ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Seimu]] (131-190 AD), but nothing is known of its history prior to its appearance in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records in the early [[Heian period]]. The shrine also claims that it was originally located on Mount Hongu to the north of the present site, and was relocated in 807 by [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]]. As the worship of [[Hachiman]] spread in the late Heian period, [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] had a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] installed at this shrine in 1062, which was thereafter referred to as the {{nihongo|Nissaka Hachiman-gu|日坂八幡宮||}} or simply {{nihongo|Hachiman-gu|八幡宮||}}. It has been referred to as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Tōtōmi Province since the early Kamakura period. As the shrine of located near [[Nissaka-shuku]] on the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway near the location of the feared Nakayama Pass, one of the three major obstacles on the Tōkaidō, travelers would make prayers and offerings at this shrine. This is mentioned many times in literature, from [[Sei Shōnagon]]'s ''[[The Pillow Book]]'', the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]] and the [[Jippensha Ikku]]'s ''[[Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige]]'' of the [[Edo Period]]. It was restored by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1603, who gave it estates worth 100 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The ancient name of "Kotonomama" was only revived after the end of [[World War II]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 5.jpg|Second Torii 事任八幡宮 拝殿.jpg|Heiden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The ancient name of "Kotonomama" was only revived after the end of [[World War II]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 5.jpg|Second Torii 事任八幡宮 拝殿.jpg|Heiden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kakegawa, Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kotonomama Hachiman-gū
事任八幡宮 | image = Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 2.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Kotonomama Hachiman-gū {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|47|54.20|N|138|04|31.54|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kotonomachi-hime no mikoto
[[Hachiman]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Seimu]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 642 Yasaka, Kakegawa-shi, Shizuoka-ken 436-0004 | website = {{Official|1=http://kotonomama.org}} | architecture_style = | festival = three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotonomama Hachiman-gū'''|事任八幡宮||}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]], the other being the [[Oguni Jinja]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]][http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The main ''[[kami]]'' at this shrine is {{nihongo|'''Kotonomachi-hime no mikoto'''|己等乃麻知媛命||}}, the mother of [[Ame-no-Koyane]] and the ancestor of the [[Nakatomi clan]], and the [[Fujiwara clan]]. However, as the one of the shrine implies, the main focus of the shrine is on the [[Hachiman]] trinity, which at this shrine are identified as [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ōjin]] and [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The foundation date of this shrine is unknown. The shrine's legend states that it was established by the [[Mononobe clan]], who were the ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Seimu]] (131-190 AD), but nothing is known of its history prior to its appearance in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records in the early [[Heian period]]. The shrine also claims that it was originally located on Mount Hongu to the north of the present site, and was relocated in 807 by [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]]. As the worship of [[Hachiman]] spread in the late Heian period, [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] had a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] installed at this shrine in 1062, which was thereafter referred to as the {{nihongo|Nissaka Hachiman-gu|日坂八幡宮||}} or simply {{nihongo|Hachiman-gu|八幡宮||}}. It has been referred to as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Tōtōmi Province since the early Kamakura period. As the shrine of located near [[Nissaka-shuku]] on the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway near the location of the feared Nakayama Pass, one of the three major obstacles on the Tōkaidō, travelers would make prayers and offerings at this shrine. This is mentioned many times in literature, from [[Sei Shōnagon]]'s ''[[The Pillow Book]]'', the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]] and the [[Jippensha Ikku]]'s ''[[Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige]]'' of the [[Edo Period]]. It was restored by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1603, who gave it estates worth 100 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The ancient name of "Kotonomama" was only revived after the end of [[World War II]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 5.jpg|Second Torii 事任八幡宮 拝殿.jpg|Heiden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] The shrine also claims that it was originally located on Mount Hongu to the north of the present site, and was relocated in 807 by [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]]. As the worship of [[Hachiman]] spread in the late Heian period, [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] had a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] installed at this shrine in 1062, which was thereafter referred to as the {{nihongo|Nissaka Hachiman-gu|日坂八幡宮||}} or simply {{nihongo|Hachiman-gu|八幡宮||}}. It has been referred to as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Tōtōmi Province since the early Kamakura period. As the shrine of located near [[Nissaka-shuku]] on the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway near the location of the feared Nakayama Pass, one of the three major obstacles on the Tōkaidō, travelers would make prayers and offerings at this shrine. This is mentioned many times in literature, from [[Sei Shōnagon]]'s ''[[The Pillow Book]]'', the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]] and the [[Jippensha Ikku]]'s ''[[Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige]]'' of the [[Edo Period]]. It was restored by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1603, who gave it estates worth 100 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The ancient name of "Kotonomama" was only revived after the end of [[World War II]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 5.jpg|Second Torii 事任八幡宮 拝殿.jpg|Heiden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kakegawa, Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kotonomama Hachiman-gū
事任八幡宮 | image = Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 2.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Kotonomama Hachiman-gū {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|47|54.20|N|138|04|31.54|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kotonomachi-hime no mikoto
[[Hachiman]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Seimu]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 642 Yasaka, Kakegawa-shi, Shizuoka-ken 436-0004 | website = {{Official|1=http://kotonomama.org}} | architecture_style = | festival = three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotonomama Hachiman-gū'''|事任八幡宮||}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]], the other being the [[Oguni Jinja]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]][http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The main ''[[kami]]'' at this shrine is {{nihongo|'''Kotonomachi-hime no mikoto'''|己等乃麻知媛命||}}, the mother of [[Ame-no-Koyane]] and the ancestor of the [[Nakatomi clan]], and the [[Fujiwara clan]]. However, as the one of the shrine implies, the main focus of the shrine is on the [[Hachiman]] trinity, which at this shrine are identified as [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ōjin]] and [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The foundation date of this shrine is unknown. The shrine's legend states that it was established by the [[Mononobe clan]], who were the ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Seimu]] (131-190 AD), but nothing is known of its history prior to its appearance in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records in the early [[Heian period]]. The shrine also claims that it was originally located on Mount Hongu to the north of the present site, and was relocated in 807 by [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]]. As the worship of [[Hachiman]] spread in the late Heian period, [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] had a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] installed at this shrine in 1062, which was thereafter referred to as the {{nihongo|Nissaka Hachiman-gu|日坂八幡宮||}} or simply {{nihongo|Hachiman-gu|八幡宮||}}. It has been referred to as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Tōtōmi Province since the early Kamakura period. As the shrine of located near [[Nissaka-shuku]] on the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway near the location of the feared Nakayama Pass, one of the three major obstacles on the Tōkaidō, travelers would make prayers and offerings at this shrine. This is mentioned many times in literature, from [[Sei Shōnagon]]'s ''[[The Pillow Book]]'', the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]] and the [[Jippensha Ikku]]'s ''[[Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige]]'' of the [[Edo Period]]. It was restored by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1603, who gave it estates worth 100 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The ancient name of "Kotonomama" was only revived after the end of [[World War II]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 5.jpg|Second Torii 事任八幡宮 拝殿.jpg|Heiden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kakegawa, Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kotonomama Hachiman-gū
事任八幡宮 | image = Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 2.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Kotonomama Hachiman-gū {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|47|54.20|N|138|04|31.54|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kotonomachi-hime no mikoto
[[Hachiman]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Seimu]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 642 Yasaka, Kakegawa-shi, Shizuoka-ken 436-0004 | website = {{Official|1=http://kotonomama.org}} | architecture_style = | festival = three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotonomama Hachiman-gū'''|事任八幡宮||}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]], the other being the [[Oguni Jinja]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]][http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The main ''[[kami]]'' at this shrine is {{nihongo|'''Kotonomachi-hime no mikoto'''|己等乃麻知媛命||}}, the mother of [[Ame-no-Koyane]] and the ancestor of the [[Nakatomi clan]], and the [[Fujiwara clan]]. However, as the one of the shrine implies, the main focus of the shrine is on the [[Hachiman]] trinity, which at this shrine are identified as [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ōjin]] and [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The foundation date of this shrine is unknown. The shrine's legend states that it was established by the [[Mononobe clan]], who were the ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Seimu]] (131-190 AD), but nothing is known of its history prior to its appearance in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records in the early [[Heian period]]. The shrine also claims that it was originally located on Mount Hongu to the north of the present site, and was relocated in 807 by [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]]. As the worship of [[Hachiman]] spread in the late Heian period, [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] had a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] installed at this shrine in 1062, which was thereafter referred to as the {{nihongo|Nissaka Hachiman-gu|日坂八幡宮||}} or simply {{nihongo|Hachiman-gu|八幡宮||}}. It has been referred to as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Tōtōmi Province since the early Kamakura period. As the shrine of located near [[Nissaka-shuku]] on the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway near the location of the feared Nakayama Pass, one of the three major obstacles on the Tōkaidō, travelers would make prayers and offerings at this shrine. This is mentioned many times in literature, from [[Sei Shōnagon]]'s ''[[The Pillow Book]]'', the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]] and the [[Jippensha Ikku]]'s ''[[Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige]]'' of the [[Edo Period]]. It was restored by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1603, who gave it estates worth 100 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The ancient name of "Kotonomama" was only revived after the end of [[World War II]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 5.jpg|Second Torii 事任八幡宮 拝殿.jpg|Heiden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kakegawa, Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kotonomama Hachiman-gū
事任八幡宮 | image = Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 2.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Kotonomama Hachiman-gū {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|47|54.20|N|138|04|31.54|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kotonomachi-hime no mikoto
[[Hachiman]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Seimu]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 642 Yasaka, Kakegawa-shi, Shizuoka-ken 436-0004 | website = {{Official|1=http://kotonomama.org}} | architecture_style = | festival = three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotonomama Hachiman-gū'''|事任八幡宮||}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]], the other being the [[Oguni Jinja]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]][http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The main ''[[kami]]'' at this shrine is {{nihongo|'''Kotonomachi-hime no mikoto'''|己等乃麻知媛命||}}, the mother of [[Ame-no-Koyane]] and the ancestor of the [[Nakatomi clan]], and the [[Fujiwara clan]]. However, as the one of the shrine implies, the main focus of the shrine is on the [[Hachiman]] trinity, which at this shrine are identified as [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ōjin]] and [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The foundation date of this shrine is unknown. The shrine's legend states that it was established by the [[Mononobe clan]], who were the ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Seimu]] (131-190 AD), but nothing is known of its history prior to its appearance in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records in the early [[Heian period]]. The shrine also claims that it was originally located on Mount Hongu to the north of the present site, and was relocated in 807 by [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]]. As the worship of [[Hachiman]] spread in the late Heian period, [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] had a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] installed at this shrine in 1062, which was thereafter referred to as the {{nihongo|Nissaka Hachiman-gu|日坂八幡宮||}} or simply {{nihongo|Hachiman-gu|八幡宮||}}. It has been referred to as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Tōtōmi Province since the early Kamakura period. As the shrine of located near [[Nissaka-shuku]] on the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway near the location of the feared Nakayama Pass, one of the three major obstacles on the Tōkaidō, travelers would make prayers and offerings at this shrine. This is mentioned many times in literature, from [[Sei Shōnagon]]'s ''[[The Pillow Book]]'', the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]] and the [[Jippensha Ikku]]'s ''[[Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige]]'' of the [[Edo Period]]. It was restored by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1603, who gave it estates worth 100 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The ancient name of "Kotonomama" was only revived after the end of [[World War II]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 5.jpg|Second Torii 事任八幡宮 拝殿.jpg|Heiden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Seimu]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 642 Yasaka, Kakegawa-shi, Shizuoka-ken 436-0004 | website = {{Official|1=http://kotonomama.org}} | architecture_style = | festival = three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotonomama Hachiman-gū'''|事任八幡宮||}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]], the other being the [[Oguni Jinja]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]][http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The main ''[[kami]]'' at this shrine is {{nihongo|'''Kotonomachi-hime no mikoto'''|己等乃麻知媛命||}}, the mother of [[Ame-no-Koyane]] and the ancestor of the [[Nakatomi clan]], and the [[Fujiwara clan]]. However, as the one of the shrine implies, the main focus of the shrine is on the [[Hachiman]] trinity, which at this shrine are identified as [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ōjin]] and [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The foundation date of this shrine is unknown. The shrine's legend states that it was established by the [[Mononobe clan]], who were the ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Seimu]] (131-190 AD), but nothing is known of its history prior to its appearance in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records in the early [[Heian period]]. The shrine also claims that it was originally located on Mount Hongu to the north of the present site, and was relocated in 807 by [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]]. As the worship of [[Hachiman]] spread in the late Heian period, [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] had a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] installed at this shrine in 1062, which was thereafter referred to as the {{nihongo|Nissaka Hachiman-gu|日坂八幡宮||}} or simply {{nihongo|Hachiman-gu|八幡宮||}}. It has been referred to as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Tōtōmi Province since the early Kamakura period. As the shrine of located near [[Nissaka-shuku]] on the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway near the location of the feared Nakayama Pass, one of the three major obstacles on the Tōkaidō, travelers would make prayers and offerings at this shrine. This is mentioned many times in literature, from [[Sei Shōnagon]]'s ''[[The Pillow Book]]'', the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]] and the [[Jippensha Ikku]]'s ''[[Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige]]'' of the [[Edo Period]]. It was restored by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1603, who gave it estates worth 100 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The ancient name of "Kotonomama" was only revived after the end of [[World War II]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 5.jpg|Second Torii 事任八幡宮 拝殿.jpg|Heiden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kakegawa, Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kotonomama Hachiman-gū
事任八幡宮 | image = Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 2.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Kotonomama Hachiman-gū {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|47|54.20|N|138|04|31.54|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kotonomachi-hime no mikoto
[[Hachiman]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Seimu]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 642 Yasaka, Kakegawa-shi, Shizuoka-ken 436-0004 | website = {{Official|1=http://kotonomama.org}} | architecture_style = | festival = three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotonomama Hachiman-gū'''|事任八幡宮||}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]], the other being the [[Oguni Jinja]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]][http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The main ''[[kami]]'' at this shrine is {{nihongo|'''Kotonomachi-hime no mikoto'''|己等乃麻知媛命||}}, the mother of [[Ame-no-Koyane]] and the ancestor of the [[Nakatomi clan]], and the [[Fujiwara clan]]. However, as the one of the shrine implies, the main focus of the shrine is on the [[Hachiman]] trinity, which at this shrine are identified as [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ōjin]] and [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The foundation date of this shrine is unknown. The shrine's legend states that it was established by the [[Mononobe clan]], who were the ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Seimu]] (131-190 AD), but nothing is known of its history prior to its appearance in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records in the early [[Heian period]]. The shrine also claims that it was originally located on Mount Hongu to the north of the present site, and was relocated in 807 by [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]]. As the worship of [[Hachiman]] spread in the late Heian period, [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] had a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] installed at this shrine in 1062, which was thereafter referred to as the {{nihongo|Nissaka Hachiman-gu|日坂八幡宮||}} or simply {{nihongo|Hachiman-gu|八幡宮||}}. It has been referred to as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Tōtōmi Province since the early Kamakura period. As the shrine of located near [[Nissaka-shuku]] on the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway near the location of the feared Nakayama Pass, one of the three major obstacles on the Tōkaidō, travelers would make prayers and offerings at this shrine. This is mentioned many times in literature, from [[Sei Shōnagon]]'s ''[[The Pillow Book]]'', the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]] and the [[Jippensha Ikku]]'s ''[[Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige]]'' of the [[Edo Period]]. It was restored by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1603, who gave it estates worth 100 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The ancient name of "Kotonomama" was only revived after the end of [[World War II]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 5.jpg|Second Torii 事任八幡宮 拝殿.jpg|Heiden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] As the worship of [[Hachiman]] spread in the late Heian period, [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] had a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] installed at this shrine in 1062, which was thereafter referred to as the {{nihongo|Nissaka Hachiman-gu|日坂八幡宮||}} or simply {{nihongo|Hachiman-gu|八幡宮||}}. It has been referred to as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Tōtōmi Province since the early Kamakura period. As the shrine of located near [[Nissaka-shuku]] on the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway near the location of the feared Nakayama Pass, one of the three major obstacles on the Tōkaidō, travelers would make prayers and offerings at this shrine. This is mentioned many times in literature, from [[Sei Shōnagon]]'s ''[[The Pillow Book]]'', the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]] and the [[Jippensha Ikku]]'s ''[[Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige]]'' of the [[Edo Period]]. It was restored by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1603, who gave it estates worth 100 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The ancient name of "Kotonomama" was only revived after the end of [[World War II]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 5.jpg|Second Torii 事任八幡宮 拝殿.jpg|Heiden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kakegawa, Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kotonomama Hachiman-gū
事任八幡宮 | image = Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 2.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Kotonomama Hachiman-gū {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|47|54.20|N|138|04|31.54|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kotonomachi-hime no mikoto
[[Hachiman]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Seimu]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 642 Yasaka, Kakegawa-shi, Shizuoka-ken 436-0004 | website = {{Official|1=http://kotonomama.org}} | architecture_style = | festival = three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotonomama Hachiman-gū'''|事任八幡宮||}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]], the other being the [[Oguni Jinja]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]][http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The main ''[[kami]]'' at this shrine is {{nihongo|'''Kotonomachi-hime no mikoto'''|己等乃麻知媛命||}}, the mother of [[Ame-no-Koyane]] and the ancestor of the [[Nakatomi clan]], and the [[Fujiwara clan]]. However, as the one of the shrine implies, the main focus of the shrine is on the [[Hachiman]] trinity, which at this shrine are identified as [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ōjin]] and [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The foundation date of this shrine is unknown. The shrine's legend states that it was established by the [[Mononobe clan]], who were the ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Seimu]] (131-190 AD), but nothing is known of its history prior to its appearance in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records in the early [[Heian period]]. The shrine also claims that it was originally located on Mount Hongu to the north of the present site, and was relocated in 807 by [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]]. As the worship of [[Hachiman]] spread in the late Heian period, [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] had a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] installed at this shrine in 1062, which was thereafter referred to as the {{nihongo|Nissaka Hachiman-gu|日坂八幡宮||}} or simply {{nihongo|Hachiman-gu|八幡宮||}}. It has been referred to as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Tōtōmi Province since the early Kamakura period. As the shrine of located near [[Nissaka-shuku]] on the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway near the location of the feared Nakayama Pass, one of the three major obstacles on the Tōkaidō, travelers would make prayers and offerings at this shrine. This is mentioned many times in literature, from [[Sei Shōnagon]]'s ''[[The Pillow Book]]'', the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]] and the [[Jippensha Ikku]]'s ''[[Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige]]'' of the [[Edo Period]]. It was restored by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1603, who gave it estates worth 100 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The ancient name of "Kotonomama" was only revived after the end of [[World War II]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 5.jpg|Second Torii 事任八幡宮 拝殿.jpg|Heiden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kakegawa, Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kotonomama Hachiman-gū
事任八幡宮 | image = Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 2.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Kotonomama Hachiman-gū {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|47|54.20|N|138|04|31.54|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kotonomachi-hime no mikoto
[[Hachiman]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Seimu]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 642 Yasaka, Kakegawa-shi, Shizuoka-ken 436-0004 | website = {{Official|1=http://kotonomama.org}} | architecture_style = | festival = three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotonomama Hachiman-gū'''|事任八幡宮||}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]], the other being the [[Oguni Jinja]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]][http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The main ''[[kami]]'' at this shrine is {{nihongo|'''Kotonomachi-hime no mikoto'''|己等乃麻知媛命||}}, the mother of [[Ame-no-Koyane]] and the ancestor of the [[Nakatomi clan]], and the [[Fujiwara clan]]. However, as the one of the shrine implies, the main focus of the shrine is on the [[Hachiman]] trinity, which at this shrine are identified as [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ōjin]] and [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The foundation date of this shrine is unknown. The shrine's legend states that it was established by the [[Mononobe clan]], who were the ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Seimu]] (131-190 AD), but nothing is known of its history prior to its appearance in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records in the early [[Heian period]]. The shrine also claims that it was originally located on Mount Hongu to the north of the present site, and was relocated in 807 by [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]]. As the worship of [[Hachiman]] spread in the late Heian period, [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] had a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] installed at this shrine in 1062, which was thereafter referred to as the {{nihongo|Nissaka Hachiman-gu|日坂八幡宮||}} or simply {{nihongo|Hachiman-gu|八幡宮||}}. It has been referred to as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Tōtōmi Province since the early Kamakura period. As the shrine of located near [[Nissaka-shuku]] on the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway near the location of the feared Nakayama Pass, one of the three major obstacles on the Tōkaidō, travelers would make prayers and offerings at this shrine. This is mentioned many times in literature, from [[Sei Shōnagon]]'s ''[[The Pillow Book]]'', the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]] and the [[Jippensha Ikku]]'s ''[[Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige]]'' of the [[Edo Period]]. It was restored by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1603, who gave it estates worth 100 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The ancient name of "Kotonomama" was only revived after the end of [[World War II]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 5.jpg|Second Torii 事任八幡宮 拝殿.jpg|Heiden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 5.jpg|Second Torii 事任八幡宮 拝殿.jpg|Heiden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kakegawa, Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kotonomama Hachiman-gū
事任八幡宮 | image = Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 2.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Kotonomama Hachiman-gū {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|47|54.20|N|138|04|31.54|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kotonomachi-hime no mikoto
[[Hachiman]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Seimu]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 642 Yasaka, Kakegawa-shi, Shizuoka-ken 436-0004 | website = {{Official|1=http://kotonomama.org}} | architecture_style = | festival = three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotonomama Hachiman-gū'''|事任八幡宮||}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]], the other being the [[Oguni Jinja]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]][http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The main ''[[kami]]'' at this shrine is {{nihongo|'''Kotonomachi-hime no mikoto'''|己等乃麻知媛命||}}, the mother of [[Ame-no-Koyane]] and the ancestor of the [[Nakatomi clan]], and the [[Fujiwara clan]]. However, as the one of the shrine implies, the main focus of the shrine is on the [[Hachiman]] trinity, which at this shrine are identified as [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ōjin]] and [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The foundation date of this shrine is unknown. The shrine's legend states that it was established by the [[Mononobe clan]], who were the ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Seimu]] (131-190 AD), but nothing is known of its history prior to its appearance in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records in the early [[Heian period]]. The shrine also claims that it was originally located on Mount Hongu to the north of the present site, and was relocated in 807 by [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]]. As the worship of [[Hachiman]] spread in the late Heian period, [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] had a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] installed at this shrine in 1062, which was thereafter referred to as the {{nihongo|Nissaka Hachiman-gu|日坂八幡宮||}} or simply {{nihongo|Hachiman-gu|八幡宮||}}. It has been referred to as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Tōtōmi Province since the early Kamakura period. As the shrine of located near [[Nissaka-shuku]] on the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway near the location of the feared Nakayama Pass, one of the three major obstacles on the Tōkaidō, travelers would make prayers and offerings at this shrine. This is mentioned many times in literature, from [[Sei Shōnagon]]'s ''[[The Pillow Book]]'', the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]] and the [[Jippensha Ikku]]'s ''[[Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige]]'' of the [[Edo Period]]. It was restored by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1603, who gave it estates worth 100 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The ancient name of "Kotonomama" was only revived after the end of [[World War II]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 5.jpg|Second Torii 事任八幡宮 拝殿.jpg|Heiden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kakegawa, Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kotonomama Hachiman-gū
事任八幡宮 | image = Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 2.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Kotonomama Hachiman-gū {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|47|54.20|N|138|04|31.54|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kotonomachi-hime no mikoto
[[Hachiman]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Seimu]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 642 Yasaka, Kakegawa-shi, Shizuoka-ken 436-0004 | website = {{Official|1=http://kotonomama.org}} | architecture_style = | festival = three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotonomama Hachiman-gū'''|事任八幡宮||}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]], the other being the [[Oguni Jinja]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]][http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually three days prior to [[Respect for the Aged Day]] in September.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The main ''[[kami]]'' at this shrine is {{nihongo|'''Kotonomachi-hime no mikoto'''|己等乃麻知媛命||}}, the mother of [[Ame-no-Koyane]] and the ancestor of the [[Nakatomi clan]], and the [[Fujiwara clan]]. However, as the one of the shrine implies, the main focus of the shrine is on the [[Hachiman]] trinity, which at this shrine are identified as [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ōjin]] and [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The foundation date of this shrine is unknown. The shrine's legend states that it was established by the [[Mononobe clan]], who were the ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Seimu]] (131-190 AD), but nothing is known of its history prior to its appearance in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records in the early [[Heian period]]. The shrine also claims that it was originally located on Mount Hongu to the north of the present site, and was relocated in 807 by [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]]. As the worship of [[Hachiman]] spread in the late Heian period, [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] had a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] installed at this shrine in 1062, which was thereafter referred to as the {{nihongo|Nissaka Hachiman-gu|日坂八幡宮||}} or simply {{nihongo|Hachiman-gu|八幡宮||}}. It has been referred to as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Tōtōmi Province since the early Kamakura period. As the shrine of located near [[Nissaka-shuku]] on the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway near the location of the feared Nakayama Pass, one of the three major obstacles on the Tōkaidō, travelers would make prayers and offerings at this shrine. This is mentioned many times in literature, from [[Sei Shōnagon]]'s ''[[The Pillow Book]]'', the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]] and the [[Jippensha Ikku]]'s ''[[Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige]]'' of the [[Edo Period]]. It was restored by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1603, who gave it estates worth 100 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The ancient name of "Kotonomama" was only revived after the end of [[World War II]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kotonomama Hachimangu 201708late 5.jpg|Second Torii 事任八幡宮 拝殿.jpg|Heiden ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]]
